content.full_text:
State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 207 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

November 30, 1976


Floyd Shimomura                                          76-11-119
Deputy Attorney General
555 Capitol Mall, Suite 550
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Floyd:

     This letter is in response to your request for comments
on Opinion No. CV 76/203 concerning whether Commissioner Richard
Little of the Central Coast Regional Coastal Commission may make,
participate in making or use his official position to influence
decisions which may affect two clients of his law firm. Initially,
we request that the Attorney General's response be contained in an
informal indexed letter rather than in a formal published opinion.

     The facts to which we address our response, as we understand them, are as follows: Mr. Little is a Commissioner with the
Central Coast Regional Coastal Commission and is an attorney who
owns 25% of the stock of his law firm, which is a professional corporation. Two of the law firm's clients frequently appear before
the Central Coast Regional Coastal Commission in support of or in
opposition to matters upon which Mr. Little must render a decision
in his capacity as a Coastal Commissioner. Client 1 retains the
law firm for purposes totally unrelated to the activities of the
Coastal Commission. Moreover, at the present time, Client 1 has
paid Mr. Little's firm the sum of $161 in legal fees and it is
anticipated that Client 1 will pay the firm an additional $100 before the end of the year. Client 2 represents persons or entities
that have interests in construction and real estate development
within the permit area. Thus far, Mr. Little and his law firm
have been consulted by Client 2 only on matters related to Client
2's corporate activities and business affairs which are unrelated
to the permit area. At present, Client 2 has paid the firm the
sum of $50 and it is anticipated that Client 2 will pay an additional $1,000 before the end of the year.

     The request letter stipulates that pursuant to Government Code Section 87103-1/ the law firm is an investment and
a source of income to Mr. Little. With respect to the applicable

1/All statutory references are to the Government Code unless
otherwise noted.

Floyd Shimomura
Page Two
November 30, 1976

standards for assessing materiality set forth in 2 Cal. Adm. Code
Section 18702, the request provides the following information:
Should one of the clients terminate its relationship with the law
firm, the effect would be to decrease the annual gross revenue of
the firm by less than 1% and/or the annual net income of the firm
by less than 5%; and no decision of the Coastal Commission ever
could, directly or indirectly, increase or decrease the assets of
the firm by $50,000 or 5% of the current assets.

There are three problems with this information. First,
the applicable standard is whether the net income and assets of
the firm would be affected by one-half of 1% (.5%) rather than 5%.
Second, the letter does not discuss whether a decision might cause
both clients to terminate their relationships with their firm simultaneously. Third, there is no information provided concerning
whether a decision of the Coastal Commission ever would "foreseeably"
cause one or both of the clients to terminate their relationships
with the firm.

In any event, if you ultimately conclude that it is
reasonably foreseeable that a decision of the Coastal Commission
will have a material financial effect on the firm, Mr. Little
should refrain from making, participating in making or using his
official position to influence such a decision. However, based
on the facts provided, it seems unlikely that a Coastal Commission
decision would have such an effect.

The request letter also does not discuss whether the
clients themselves might represent sources of income to Mr. Little.
Section 82030 provides that the income of an individual also includes a pro rata share of the income of a business entity in which
the filer owns directly, indirectly or beneficially a 10% interest
or greater. In the instant case, the request letter states that
Mr. Little has a 25% interest in his law firm. Accordingly, a client will be a source of income to Mr. Little within the meaning of
Section 87103, if his pro rata share of the client's payments to
the firm equals $250 or more.

Client 1 does not appear to be a source of income to
Mr. Little pursuant to this standard even if the amount which has
been received and which will be received are totaled. However,
Client 2, if the receivd and expected fees are added together,
conceivably could be a source of income to Mr. Little. Nevertheless, it is my understanding that your opinion will not reach the
question of whether expected income may be included for the purpose
of determining whether a client is a source of income. Rather,
the opinion will be limited to a recitation of the standards set
forth in Section 87103 and will not contain an interpretation of
those standards. If you do decide to interpret the provisions of
Section 87103, we would appreciate the opportunity to submit our
views at that time.

Page Three
November 30, 1976

Even if expected income is not considered, it is con-
ceivable that Client 2 will have paid or have promised to pay the
firm $1,000 (of which Mr. Little's pro rata share would be $250)
by the time your opinion is issued. In that event, Client 2 would
be a source of income to Mr. Little. However, in order for a source
of income to be a disqualifying "financial interest" within the
meaning of Sections 87100 and 87103, it must be reasonably, forseee-
able that a decision by Mr. Little will have a "material financial
effect" on the source of income.

Guidelines for determining when a decision has a "material
financial effect" on a source of income are set forth in 2 Cal.
Adm. Code Section 18702. However, we have not been given facts
which would enable us to apply these guidelines and to determine
whether it is reasonably foreseeable that any decision of the
Coastal Commission will materially affect Client 2. If you acquire
such facts, we will be happy to submit our views as to whether ma-
teriality exists. If you should ultimately determine that Client
2 is a source of income to Mr. Little and that a decision foresee-
ably would materially affect Client 2, Mr. Little should not make,
participate in the making, or use his official position to influ-
ence such a decision.

Lastly, the request letter asks whether a client would
be a source of income to Mr. Little if he did not personally handle
any of the client's business and if his interest in the professional
corporation were modified to provide that he would not receive any
income, directly or indirectly, from fees paid by the client. Un-
der these circumstances, we believe that the client in question
probably would not constitute a source of income to Mr. Little
since he would neither serve the client nor receive any benefit,
either directly or indirectly, from that client's association with
the law firm.

Section 82030 provides "income of an individual also in-
cludes a pro rata share of any income of any business entity or
trust in which the individual or spouse owns, directly, indirectly or beneficially, a ten percent interest or greater. (Emphasis added) Under the facts described above, Mr. Little would not
appear to have any pro rata share in the business entity's income
from the client's fees. Accordingly, such fees would not be income
to Mr. Little and the client, therefore, would not be a source of
income to him. We note parenthetically, however, that if other
circumstances (e.g., if the arrangement were created for the purpose of evading the provisions of the Political Reform Act), we
might reach a different result.

If you have any further questions please contact either
me or Ted Prim, an attorney with the Commission's Legal Division.

Sincerely,

[signature]

Michael Bennett
Executive Director
